title: Circle Area and Perimeter Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate the area (πr²) and circumference of any circle instantly. Free tool with step-by-step formulas.
intro: The Circle Area and Perimeter Calculator computes three fundamental measurements of a circle from a single input: its radius (r). **Area** measures the total surface enclosed by the circle — calculated as A = π · r² — while **Perimeter** (also called circumference) measures the total length of the circle's boundary — calculated as C = 2 · π · r. The **Diameter** is simply D = 2r. Enter any radius and instantly get all three values. These formulas are foundational in geometry, engineering, architecture, and everyday tasks such as sizing pipes, circular plots of land, wheels, or pizza pans. The constant π ≈ 3.14159265358979 links every circle's dimensions universally.
keyTakeaway: For a circle with radius r: **Area = π · r² ≈ 3.14159 · r²** and **Circumference = 2 · π · r ≈ 6.28318 · r**. Example: r = 5 cm → Area = 78.54 cm², Circumference = 31.42 cm, Diameter = 10 cm.
useCases: Calculating the area of a circular garden bed or irrigation zone to determine how many square feet of mulch, sod, or fertilizer to purchase.
Determining the circumference of a wheel or tire to compute distance traveled per revolution in automotive or bicycle engineering.
Sizing circular pipes, tanks, or silos in plumbing and civil engineering — cross-sectional area directly affects flow rate (Q = A · v).
Finding the material needed to cut circular pieces from sheet metal, fabric, glass, or plywood — minimizing waste in manufacturing.
Computing the footprint of a round table, fountain, or column base for interior design or construction layout purposes.
explanation:
## How It Is Calculated

All three outputs derive from a single input: the **radius r** (the distance from the center of the circle to any point on its boundary).

```
Diameter   D = 2 · r
Area       A = π · r²
Perimeter  C = 2 · π · r   (also written as π · D)

where π ≈ 3.14159265358979...
```

These formulas are exact for a perfect (Euclidean) circle. In practice, calculators use IEEE 754 double-precision floating-point arithmetic, giving results accurate to ~15 significant digits.

---

## Reference Table

Below are pre-computed values for common radii to help you cross-check results quickly.

| Radius (r) | Diameter (D) | Area (A) | Circumference (C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 cm | 2 cm | 3.1416 cm² | 6.2832 cm |
| 2 cm | 4 cm | 12.5664 cm² | 12.5664 cm |
| 5 cm | 10 cm | 78.5398 cm² | 31.4159 cm |
| 10 cm | 20 cm | 314.1593 cm² | 62.8318 cm |
| 25 cm | 50 cm | 1,963.4954 cm² | 157.0796 cm |
| 1 ft | 2 ft | 3.1416 ft² | 6.2832 ft |
| 3 ft | 6 ft | 28.2743 ft² | 18.8496 ft |
| 6 ft | 12 ft | 113.0973 ft² | 37.6991 ft |
| 10 ft | 20 ft | 314.1593 ft² | 62.8318 ft |
| 1 m | 2 m | 3.1416 m² | 6.2832 m |
| 5 m | 10 m | 78.5398 m² | 31.4159 m |

> **Tip:** Area scales with r² — doubling the radius **quadruples** the area. Circumference scales linearly — doubling the radius **doubles** the circumference.

---

## Typical Use Cases With Numbers

### Example 1 — Circular Patio (r = 5 ft)
A homeowner wants a round concrete patio with a radius of 5 feet.

```
A = π · 5² = π · 25 ≈ 78.54 ft²
C = 2 · π · 5 ≈ 31.42 ft  (edging/border length)
D = 10 ft
```
At a concrete cost of ~$6/ft², the slab would cost roughly **$471**. The decorative border at $4/linear ft would add **~$126**.

### Example 2 — Bicycle Wheel (r = 13.5 in)
A standard 27-inch road bicycle wheel has a radius of 13.5 inches.

```
C = 2 · π · 13.5 ≈ 84.82 in ≈ 7.07 ft per revolution
```
At 90 RPM cadence on a 1:1 gear ratio, that equals **~6.36 ft/s ≈ 4.3 mph** of linear speed — a useful check for speedometer calibration.

### Example 3 — Irrigation Circle (r = 20 m)
A center-pivot irrigation system covers a circle of radius 20 m.

```
A = π · 20² = π · 400 ≈ 1,256.64 m² ≈ 0.1257 hectares
```
At a water application rate of 5 mm/hr, the system delivers **~6,283 liters per hour** to that plot.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Confusing radius and diameter** — The most frequent error. If you measure across the circle, you have the diameter; you must divide by 2 before entering it as the radius. Using D instead of r inflates the area by **4×**.

2. **Using π ≈ 3.14 instead of full precision** — For small radii the error is negligible, but for r = 100 m, using 3.14 gives an area of 31,400 m² vs. the correct 31,415.93 m² — a **16 m² error** that matters in land surveying.

3. **Mixing units mid-calculation** — Combining inches and centimeters without conversion corrupts every output. Always confirm that your radius is expressed in one consistent unit before computing.

4. **Forgetting that area units are squared** — If r is in meters, area is in **m²**, not meters. Reporting "78.54 cm" instead of "78.54 cm²" is dimensionally incorrect and causes errors in material estimates.

5. **Applying the formula to an ellipse or oval** — A circle has a single radius. Ovals have two semi-axes (a and b); their area is π · a · b and their perimeter requires an approximation (Ramanujan's formula). The circle formula gives wrong results for non-circular shapes.

---

faq.q: What is the formula for the area of a circle?
faq.a: The area of a circle is **A = π · r²**, where r is the radius and π ≈ 3.14159265. For example, a circle with r = 7 cm has an area of π · 49 ≈ **153.94 cm²**. The formula was proven by Archimedes (~250 BC) using the method of exhaustion, and remains exact for any perfect Euclidean circle.

faq.q: What is the difference between perimeter, circumference, and diameter?
faq.a: All three describe a circle's size but measure different things. **Diameter (D = 2r)** is the longest chord — the straight line through the center. **Circumference (C = 2πr = πD)** is the total length of the boundary curve — the 'perimeter' of the circle. 'Perimeter' and 'circumference' are synonymous for circles; circumference is the more precise geometric term.

faq.q: How do I find the radius if I only know the area?
faq.a: Rearrange the area formula: **r = √(A / π)**. For example, if A = 200 cm², then r = √(200 / 3.14159) = √(63.662) ≈ **7.98 cm**. This inverse calculation is useful when you know the desired coverage area (e.g., a sprinkler zone) and need to determine the required radius.

faq.q: How do I find the radius if I only know the circumference?
faq.a: Rearrange the circumference formula: **r = C / (2π)**. For example, if you measure a circular pipe's outer circumference as 94.25 mm, then r = 94.25 / 6.28318 ≈ **15.0 mm**, so the diameter is 30 mm. This is a standard technique in manufacturing to measure round stock without calipers.

faq.q: Why does doubling the radius quadruple the area?
faq.a: Because area depends on **r²** (radius squared). If you double r, the new area is π · (2r)² = π · 4r² = **4 times** the original. This has major practical implications: a pizza with a 12-inch diameter has **4× the area** of a 6-inch pizza, not 2×. Always think in squared terms when scaling circular areas.

faq.q: What value of π should I use for accurate calculations?
faq.a: For everyday engineering and construction, **π ≈ 3.14159** (6 significant figures) is sufficient — the error is less than 0.00001%. For high-precision scientific work, IEEE 754 double precision uses π ≈ 3.141592653589793 (16 digits). The US NIST defines π as a mathematical constant with infinite, non-repeating decimal digits; no rounding introduces a 'legal' error, but context determines acceptable precision.

faq.q: Can this calculator be used for real-world circular areas like land plots or pools?
faq.a: Yes — as long as the shape is a true circle. For a circular swimming pool with r = 4 m, A = π · 16 ≈ **50.27 m²**. Multiply by depth (e.g., 1.5 m) to get volume: **~75.4 m³ ≈ 75,400 liters**. For land measurement, the USDA and land surveyors use the same formula; 1 acre = 43,560 ft², so a circular plot of r ≈ 117.75 ft covers exactly 1 acre.

faq.q: What is the relationship between a circle's area and its circumference?
faq.a: Both are functions of r, so you can link them directly: **A = C² / (4π)** or equivalently **C = 2√(π · A)**. For instance, if C = 62.83 cm, then A = (62.83)² / (4π) = 3,947.6 / 12.566 ≈ **314.16 cm²** — matching r = 10 cm. This relationship is useful when you can only measure one quantity and need the other without computing r explicitly.

faq.q: How accurate are the results from this calculator?
faq.a: This calculator uses **IEEE 754 double-precision floating-point arithmetic**, which provides approximately 15–17 significant decimal digits of precision. For most practical applications — construction, cooking, gardening, education — results are accurate to at least 4 decimal places. The limiting factor is almost always the precision of the radius measurement you provide, not the calculation itself.
